event: The Presentation of Eve to Adam (or God Introducing Eve)
symbols: Christ-like figure in robes — God as Creator presenting Eve, or Christ as the pre-incarnate Word through whom all was made
Eve's nakedness — original innocence before the Fall, without shame
garden with fruit trees — the Garden of Eden, paradise of original blessing
blessing hand gesture — divine approval and the gift of companionship
small dark creature at their feet — possibly foreshadowing the serpent's later intrusion
description: Two figures stand together in a lush garden setting. On the left, a robed figure in a pink garment raises one hand in a gesture of blessing or instruction, with long hair and a serene expression. On the right, a pale-skinned young woman with very long blonde hair stands naked, her eyes cast downward with a modest expression. She takes or touches the hand of the robed figure. Behind them, dark trees with round fruits (possibly oranges or apples) form a backdrop. A small dark creature (possibly a rabbit) sits at their feet on the ground.
theological_description: This detail from Bosch's Garden of Earthly Delights depicts the moment when God presents Eve to Adam — or, as Bosch uniquely depicts it, a Christ-like figure introduces Eve into the created order. Genesis records: 'The Lord God made a woman from the rib he had taken out of the man, and he brought her to the man' (Genesis 2:22). The robed figure's resemblance to Christ reflects the theological understanding that the pre-incarnate Son — the Logos through whom all things were made (John 1:3) — was the agent of creation itself. Eve stands in primal innocence: 'Adam and his wife were both naked, and they felt no shame' (Genesis 2:25). The fruit-laden trees evoke the abundance of Eden, including the fateful tree of the knowledge of good and evil that would become the instrument of the Fall (Genesis 2:17). The blessing gesture suggests the divine mandate: 'Be fruitful and increase in number; fill the earth and subdue it' (Genesis 1:28). In Bosch's theological vision, this moment of original innocence and divine intention stands in stark contrast to the panels of earthly folly and hellish consequence that flank it.

scripture_refs.reason: God creating Eve from Adam's rib and presenting her — the direct narrative depicted
The original innocence and nakedness without shame of the first couple in Eden
The divine blessing and mandate to be fruitful — suggested by the blessing gesture
All things made through the Word — the Christ-like Creator figure reflects pre-incarnate Logos theology
